primary_diagnosis.name: Acute ischaemic stroke — left middle cerebral artery territory, cardioembolic (atrial fibrillation-related)

pathophysiology_summary: Untreated atrial fibrillation causes blood to stagnate in the left atrial appendage, forming a thrombus. On embolisation the clot lodges in a cerebral artery — here the left middle cerebral artery — occluding flow and producing an ischaemic penumbra around a growing infarct core. Because the dominant (usually left) hemisphere language cortex and the motor strip for the contralateral (right) face/arm/leg are supplied by the MCA, the result is the classic syndrome of sudden right facial droop, right hemiparesis and aphasia. The infarct is progressive and time-dependent: salvageable penumbra is lost each minute (‘time is brain’), which is why reperfusion — IV thrombolysis within ~4.5 hours and/or endovascular thrombectomy within ~24 hours of last-known-well — is the definitive treatment once CT excludes haemorrhage. Cessation of anticoagulation removed the protection against exactly this cardioembolic event.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden simultaneous facial droop, one-sided weakness and speech disturbance is a FAST-positive stroke syndrome until proven otherwise.
A common misattribution for facial droop — but Bell's palsy cannot explain limb weakness or aphasia and spares neither in the way seen here.
Hypoglycaemia can mimic focal deficits and is rapidly reversible — must be excluded by a glucose measurement before anchoring on stroke.
Post-ictal focal weakness can mimic stroke; less likely with no witnessed convulsion and sudden flaccid onset rather than post-ictal recovery.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the correct behaviour is to treat any sudden FAST-positive deficit as a stroke and move toward escalation, while keeping the reversible mimic (hypoglycaemia) in mind.

differential.diagnosis: acute ischaemic stroke, left MCA territory
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden onset, right face/arm/leg deficit plus aphasia localising to the left hemisphere, with atrial fibrillation and ceased anticoagulation as a strong cardioembolic source.

Clinically indistinguishable from ischaemic stroke at the bedside; recent anticoagulation use raises bleed risk. Only CT can separate them — this is why antithrombotics must be withheld until imaging.
Excluded once limb weakness and aphasia are established — a peripheral facial palsy produces none of these.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Ischaemic stroke is now leading, but haemorrhagic stroke must stay on the list because it changes what is safe to give (no aspirin) — the AI Doctor cannot distinguish them remotely.

differential.diagnosis: acute ischaemic stroke, left MCA territory (cardioembolic)
differential.evidence_basis: Normal capillary glucose excludes hypoglycaemia; irregularly irregular pulse and AF history support cardioembolism; non-contrast CT excludes haemorrhage and may show early infarct.
Excluded by CT head (hospital) — the investigation that unlocks safe thrombolysis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: These investigations are hospital-based; the telehealth role is to expedite transport so they happen fast, not to perform or await them.

differential.diagnosis: acute ischaemic stroke, left MCA territory, cardioembolic
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed syndrome within the reperfusion window; cause is embolism from AF after anticoagulation cessation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnosis is confirmed only after CT; the case is scored on time-critical recognition and escalation, not on remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: Sudden focal neurological deficit — unilateral facial droop, arm/leg weakness and speech disturbance (FAST positive)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A FAST-positive sudden deficit is an acute stroke until proven otherwise and mandates immediate emergency escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed or delayed escalation loses the reperfusion window, causing permanent, devastating disability or death.

red_flags.description: Within the reperfusion window — last known well approximately 45 minutes ago
red_flags.clinical_significance: Being early in the thrombolysis (<4.5 h) and thrombectomy (<24 h) windows makes rapid transport potentially disability-reversing.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to establish and relay the onset time can push the patient past the treatment window.

red_flags.description: Known atrial fibrillation with anticoagulation ceased — strong cardioembolic source and bleed-risk modifier
red_flags.clinical_significance: Explains the mechanism (cardioembolism) and raises haemorrhage risk if antithrombotics were given before imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring away from stroke, or giving aspirin, could cause harm.

red_flags.description: Dysphagia / aspiration risk from facial and bulbar weakness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Impaired swallow means any food or fluid risks aspiration — the patient must be kept nil by mouth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Giving food or water can cause aspiration pneumonia or acute airway compromise.

red_flags.description: Risk of declining consciousness / airway compromise as a large infarct evolves
red_flags.clinical_significance: Currently alert, but a large MCA infarct can cause deterioration and airway loss; carer must watch for it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unmonitored deterioration could lead to airway obstruction before paramedics arrive.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing the facial droop as a benign peripheral facial palsy — ignoring that limb weakness and aphasia make it a central lesion (stroke) and forehead sparing distinguishes an upper-motor-neuron pattern (a clinician-exam finding not available in telehealth).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: ‘He can still understand me, so it's mild’
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being falsely reassured because the patient follows commands — expressive aphasia with dense hemiparesis is a major stroke, not a mild event.
Advising monitoring or a next-day GP review instead of 000 — the reperfusion window closes within hours and this is the highest-cost failure mode.

Giving aspirin or any antithrombotic on the assumption it is ischaemic — haemorrhagic stroke is clinically indistinguishable and recent anticoagulation raises bleed risk; antithrombotics are withheld until CT.

Trying to ‘treat’ the raised blood pressure — permissive hypertension preserves penumbral perfusion in acute ischaemic stroke; aggressive lowering can extend the infarct and is a hospital-only decision tied to reperfusion.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Positive on all of Face, Arm and Speech; Time critical
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Any positive FAST element mandates emergency stroke pathway activation (000).

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Elevated (age, hypertension proxy, AF; this event itself is a stroke)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Confirms the patient warranted ongoing anticoagulation; cessation removed protection.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure and heart rate
Irregularly irregular pulse confirming atrial fibrillation (requires palpation/ECG)
Forehead sparing distinguishing an upper- from lower-motor-neuron facial pattern
Conjugate gaze deviation as an examined sign
Capillary blood glucose to formally exclude hypoglycaemia
Non-contrast CT / MRI head to distinguish ischaemic from haemorrhagic stroke
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance as a suspected-stroke priority to a stroke-capable centre; establish and relay the last-known-well time; keep the patient nil by mouth; recovery position on the non-paralysed (left) side if consciousness declines; withhold all antithrombotics and do not attempt BP lowering.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low risk of over-diagnosis; the dominant risk is UNDER-action — mislabelling as Bell's palsy, being reassured by preserved comprehension, or delaying to gather detail — plus the commission error of giving aspirin/food before hospital assessment.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Stroke Foundation (Australia) Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management 2022
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected acute stroke requires immediate emergency transport to a stroke-capable hospital; reperfusion (thrombolysis/thrombectomy) is time-critical and CT must precede antithrombotic therapy.
